item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it could not be sourced and thus is assumed to be non-compliant. As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi there! I'm from 7 years and 9 days in the future! I just found this page because I wanted to know what 1234 is! I checked 1111 before this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for a laugh more than 4 1/2 years after you posted that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was taking a look at Guilliman's map, and realized I was across the street from this SCP's supposed location yesterday.
Crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe I changed the operating dates of the restaurant back when I replaced the article photo and never got around to altering the other paragraph. It's fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I misreading this, or is it a mistake?

SCP-1234 is the former premises of "Schmidt's Swiss Pastries", a bakery and tea room that operated in Chicago, IL from 1947 to 2003

Interviews with persons who worked at SCP-1234 prior to 1973, and with the previous owners of Site 602, stated that the cause of its closing abruptly in April of that year

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It eats people en masse, which necessitates feeding it not-people stuff constantly, and one of the warnings is that it has already started eating them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't get why this is keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It doesn't really work for me. I guess the idea is to tap into the fear of something mundane and seemingly innocent could turn on you horribly but it doesn't work for me. It's a bit too goofy and I don't think the writing makes it work. Also..;

Pies produced by SCP-1234 will make use of any biological ingredients provided to it, whether living or not at time of event, including […] non-edible plants […]. SCP-1234 will not, however, make use of any ingredients that are […] of unsatisfactory condition for use in baking.

I would argue that non-edible plants would be of unsatisfactory condition for use in baking.

And one last thing…

A Foundation front company, Southside Commissary Products

Get it? because it's initials are SCP! HA HA no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for necropost, but I just had to reply…

I baked you a pie!
Oh boy, what flavour?
Pie flavour!
*guitar sound*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but the current containment protocols seem pointlessly risky.
It seems like (at least within my headcanons' views) this is well within "acceptable levels of containment." Flour and shortening can be considered "fresh" for quite some time if suitable stored, and it's easy to keep a constant on-site stock of suitable edible material (if unused, remove and feed to the D-class). Abductions nearby are, as stated, unavoidable.
In the grand scheme of things, it seems the primary risk is exposure, and the cost of life and resources (even with a constantly rotating stock) is small compared to many SCPs.
There are also measures that could be taken to render the neighborhood broadly undesirable, even to the poorest residents, and judicious use of amnestics could perhaps reduce reluctance to relocate. I can imagine Foundation engineered blight like that seen in Detroit, where entire blocks might have one or two occupied homes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a moment

SCP-1234 is the former premises of "Schmidt's Swiss Pastries", a bakery and tea room that operated in Chicago, IL from 1947 to 2003.

Foundation containment of SCP-1234 began in 2006, following several reports of dozens of persons and animals disappearing within the downtown Chicago area. […] Interviews with persons who worked at SCP-1234 prior to 1973, and with the previous owners of Site 602, stated that the cause of its closing abruptly in April of that year was not known to them and that they had never observed any unusual or anomalous events occurring within the property prior to 1996.

I'm not sure I'm clear on the timeline here but it sounds like there was a 7-10 year span there (either 1996-2003 or 1996-2006) where the anomalous effect was in place and Schmidt's was still in operation? Although I suppose that actually running the place as a bakery might be more effective at minimizing casualties than the Foundation's "evacuate and make emergency deliveries" policy - a commercial bakery would presumably have ingredients on hand, and they'd be using the ingredients and restocking often enough to avoid anything spoiling or going stale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I skimmed briefly through this SCP, and I'm wondering: why doesn't the Foundation simply keep the place sterilised and sealed from possible insects and pests, and provide it with edible food ingredients every time it's about to become active, and then sell the finished pies (if prior investigation shows no contamination) through the front company, or perhaps transport the finished, edible pies to their own cafeteria for consumption? This seems like a useful SCP if managed properly.

Edit:

  • Unexplained disappearances of humans and animals within a 300m radius of Site 602
  • [REDACTED]

I guess that explains it.

Still I can imagine that, after making sure each pie does not contain harmful ingredients, human flesh, or some other exotic animal meat, the Foundation simply serves them up at their cafeterias…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now they call it Schmidt's Crispy Pastries

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i get why its dangerous but whats keeping the foundation from setting up a bakery or hell they could even set up a baked goods shipping company nearby to provide it with fresh supplies

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I like this a lot. I only don't like the REDACTED in the bulletted list.

Also, the article doesn't mention how good the pies are. (A joke, I know that's not scientific.) Part of me wants to believe that these are the best pies ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: if that's not possible i understand why its keter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like it but what would keep the foundation from simply replacing the ingredients every day or two? maybe create a task force for it….you can call them "bakers dozen" XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's located in a heavily-populated urban area. It cannot be moved and the nearby population cannot be relocated. Its activation schedule is seemingly random. It has to be provided with fresh supplies every time it activates on said random schedule. And even when that's done, it still sometimes consumes animals and human beings in the area.

It's Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs to be classified as euclid. Classification has nothing to do with how dangerous it is. Classification is about containment (My rule of thumb is that safe means fully contained, euclid means hard or impossible to contain, and keter means it can cause a security/containment breach). The dangerous effect is easily stopped by giving it supplies, which would normally mean a safe classification. But since it's not physically contained in a foundation site, it can't be classified as safe. And it doesn't pose a security/containment breach danger, so it's not keter either. So use the "safe as long as we keep doing what we're doing" euclid classification for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: first keter scp in a while, pretty good one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation has liaisons in major police departments to look out for this sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: police called the Foundation? o_O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish I understood more of the, "random." component that allows baking to proceed. If i'm walking down the street by SCP-1234 without the containment procedures in effect, would it be possible for my whole leg to just up and vanish to be cooked by this bakery?

Do you think the foundation could take down the walls around the kitchen so they could look inside from the street? (Whilst putting up a large tent under the guise of "renovations." to prevent the public from looking in themselves.) That would certainly shed some light on this.

I take it the people who are inside that try to observe are baked.

My question is by who or what or by strange physical forces bakes these pies?

It's riled my effort enough to garner an up-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PFffTtf Hehe, Anonymous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ingredients still have to meet the bakery's standard of "freshness", and, as noted above, simply buying the building next door just isn't an option - it's hard enough to evict all the potential hundreds of tenants of a big city building when it's actually infested by vermin, or full of asbestos, or in danger of collapsing. "You have to leave your homes/places of business forever and we can't tell you why" isn't going to cut it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm.
The way you describe the effect makes it sound like if there are enough ingredients nearby the effect wont go farther than the adjacent buildings on site 602. Making it much safer than having to actually enter the building to deposit ingredients and possibly being caught in it's effect.
If the ingredients do need to be inside the bakery for maximum safety the foundation could move them in from the next door building.
This would shorten response time and make SCP-1234 less dangerous.
Would you agree this would be more efficient than bringing them in from the Southside Commissary Products building?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really not an option, considering the range of the bakery's effect. It's blackboxed in the article, but the intent is that the bakery is located in downtown Chicago, where there are thousands of human beings living and working in its area of effect at any given moment. Money might not be an object to the Foundation, but there's no way they'd be able to acquire enough real estate in the vicinity to completely keep civilians out of the danger zone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought of a way the foundation could potentially re-classify this as safe or euclid.
1. Buy and renovate the next door building into a warehouse or something similar that stores only food or baking ingredients.
2. Close off the bakery and create a secondary entrance in the warehouse building to retrieve the pies.
After that the only worry would be staying away from the bakery while it does it's work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see something good come of the number 1234.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh… it kinda reminds me of that ice cream truck.

Pretty good execution, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was told there would be pie.

I was not told that I would be IN it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly did not enjoy this. When I see Keter or Euclid buildings, I get interested because those have usually been good (uncle blackbox's redacted expunged and that nightclub with the cult are two that immediately come to mind). This…was not. The containment procedures hyped me up, and then the preparation hyped me up, and then the actual description of what happened hyped me up, and then it turned out that it only made pies. Let down of the century.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of SCP-602. In fact, I'd be willing to believe that there's a connection between them.

602 is the Sculptor, 1234 is the Piemaker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope you could come up with a better reason than "the stuff won't move" for this fine SCP. Can't they cut the hoses, or weld the doors shut on the ovens? But if that's the way it is, so it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why have attempts to remove kitchen equipment failed? What happens to prevent it?

It just won't budge. The ovens are stuck in place, the gas lines refuses to come unhooked, etc.

If humans disappear within 300 meters, why would they keep any personnel there? For that matter, why are they allowing human habitation within 300 meters of it?

Because permanently evacuating a huge swath of real estate in, say, lower Manhattan, just isn't feasible short of dropping a tactical nuke on the area. Manhattan has about 27,000 people per square kilometer, so we're talking potentially kicking over 10,000 people out of their homes and businesses and just saying "No, no reason, but you have to get out and never come back". The cost of buying out all the real estate alone would make it next to impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at "Procedure Queen-of-Hearts". A couple things, despite my upvote:

Why have attempts to remove kitchen equipment failed? What happens to prevent it?

Is it only the Foundation's monitoring equipment that is interfered with? That's how it's worded.

If humans disappear within 300 meters, why would they keep any personnel there? For that matter, why are they allowing human habitation within 300 meters of it? It's mentioned that the city it's located in has a sizable population; this just sounds like they're asking for trouble if that delivery doesn't show. This has kind of been talked about before, but I'm not satisfied with the answers.

I almost want to call this too similar to the Keter cakes (as others have), but I think the danger here is not so much the pies themselves but the gathering of ingredients. And actually it's more a melding of Keter cakes and the restaurant of doom. Still, it's written very well and overall effective. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We discussed this in the chat before I posted it. I ultimately decided to keep it Keter on the basis that;

-It's located in a heavily populated urban center (think Manhattan or London or Chicago) and can't be moved
-We have no way of predicting when it will become active (though we have enough warning to prepare when it does)
-There are disappearances associated with it even when we follow containment procedures correctly
-We don't understand and can't prevent its methods of snatching people/things and its method of delivering pies

As for just leaving flour and stuff on site, I mentioned in the article that the bakery insists on fresh ingredients - it won't use something that's just been sitting around onsite forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instances of SCP-1234-1 will not be recycled into new instances when a new event begins.

Aw, but I wanted a pie made of pie…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is also similar to SCP-002 in what it does to things, but this is good in its own right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was complaining about this exact thing to him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember Smapti asking for feedback on this last night when I was up too late in chat and being generally irresponsible and I was like "I ain't got no time or awake braincells to read a Smapti draft it's not like there is any way it could be bad" and I was right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It does. Part of why the evacuate the area.

Why don't we just leave flour and stuff on site? Why deliver it with the off chance of failure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it, actually? Because that'd make it outright uncontained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It says that one of the warning signs of an event occurring is people in the area going missing. Sounds to me like even while contained it's still grabbing people somehow. And that's definitely Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Euclid at best.
There isnt't much of a problem recontaining this one, unlike Keter cake, but if the delivery holds up somehow, whoever's nearest to it is screwed, because the effect will just keep expanding and expanding. Hell, if the surface become uninhabitable, this thing might well go and remove people from the bunkers below if they happen to be the closest suitable material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still not safe though, Euclid would be more appropiate I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm inclined to agree. Sure, it's pretty damn inimical to human life if not contained, but considering how easy it is to keep it placated, and how we're given more than adequate warning of any active events, this seems pretty easy to stop from doing anything actually dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm wondering a bit why this is Keter. Seems like as long as you give it enough flour and vegetable matter and keep nosy people from going inside it'll just make a bunch of perfectly normal pies every now and again. And since the Foundation owns the building, doesn't seem like that's much of a problem to accomplish. Maybe I'm just reading things wrong.
Still, awesome SCP, just wanted to note that one little nitpick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was bad, and you should feel bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops! Commented as a reply….dont mind me, I'll be over here derping some more. *herp de derp derp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am the Lord of the Pies. Upvote for you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was expecting a bigger payoff considering how long the build up was. Still clever enough to upvote, but I think you can trim down a lot of the pre-pie description and add some more about the pies themselves and the behavior of the store once fully stocked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty damn reminiscent on the Keter Cakes, but it's still good enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Writerbot strikes again!

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Darn those Swiss and their magical pies! Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good point. Fixed.

And yes, I did base this on the "pies appearing out of nowhere" comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You actually made the SCP that produces pies when activated? Very nice, I like it a lot. The only thing that bothered me was this:

All attempts to remove or disconnect SCP-1234's kitchen equipment or utilities have been unsuccessful.

Under normal circumstances, SCP-1234 is inert and displays no anomalous properties.

Seems to me that the kitchen equipment and utilities being impossible to remove counts as an anonymous property, and it seems to apply even when SCP-1234 is not active.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In retrospect, it does.
